969	/-! ### Polynomial-module structure from commuting endomorphisms
970	
971	A finite family `t : Fin r → End κ M` of pairwise-commuting `κ`-linear endomorphisms makes `M`
972	a module over the **free** polynomial ring `MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ`, with `X i` acting as
973	`t i`. The free polynomial ring — not the subalgebra `Algebra.adjoin κ (range t)` — is used so
974	that the inductive transfer (`subquotient_finite_transfer`) has the ring surjection
975	`MvPolynomial (Fin (r+1)) κ ↠ MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ` available; relations among the `t i`
976	inside `End κ M` would obstruct the analogous surjection of subalgebras. -/
977	
978	section PolyModule
979	
980	variable {κ M : Type*} [Field κ] [AddCommGroup M] [Module κ M]
981	
982	/-- The ring homomorphism `MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ →+* End κ M` evaluating a polynomial at a
983	pairwise-commuting family `t` of endomorphisms, factored through the commutative subalgebra
984	`Algebra.adjoin κ (range t) ⊆ End κ M` (commutative by `Algebra.isMulCommutative_adjoin`).
985	Project-local: the engine for the `MvPolynomial`-module structure on `M`. -/
986	noncomputable def polyEndHom {r : ℕ} (t : Fin r → Module.End κ M)
987	    (hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)) :
988	    MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ →+* Module.End κ M :=
989	  letI : IsMulCommutative (Algebra.adjoin κ (Set.range t)) :=
990	    Algebra.isMulCommutative_adjoin κ (by
991	      rintro _ ⟨i, rfl⟩ _ ⟨j, rfl⟩; exact (hcomm i j).eq)
992	  letI : CommRing (Algebra.adjoin κ (Set.range t)) := IsMulCommutative.instCommRing
993	  ((Algebra.adjoin κ (Set.range t)).val.toRingHom).comp
994	    (MvPolynomial.aeval
995	      (fun i => (⟨t i, Algebra.subset_adjoin (Set.mem_range_self i)⟩ :
996	        Algebra.adjoin κ (Set.range t)))).toRingHom
997	
998	/-- The evaluation ring hom sends the variable `X i` to the endomorphism `t i`. -/
999	@[simp] lemma polyEndHom_X {r : ℕ} (t : Fin r → Module.End κ M)
1000	    (hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)) (i : Fin r) :
1001	    polyEndHom t hcomm (MvPolynomial.X i) = t i := by
1002	  letI : IsMulCommutative (Algebra.adjoin κ (Set.range t)) :=
1003	    Algebra.isMulCommutative_adjoin κ (by
1004	      rintro _ ⟨i, rfl⟩ _ ⟨j, rfl⟩; exact (hcomm i j).eq)
1005	  letI : CommRing (Algebra.adjoin κ (Set.range t)) := IsMulCommutative.instCommRing
1006	  simp [polyEndHom]
1007	
1008	/-- The evaluation ring hom sends a constant `C c` to the scalar endomorphism `c • 1`. -/
1009	@[simp] lemma polyEndHom_C {r : ℕ} (t : Fin r → Module.End κ M)
1010	    (hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)) (c : κ) :
1011	    polyEndHom t hcomm (MvPolynomial.C c) = c • (1 : Module.End κ M) := by
1012	  letI : IsMulCommutative (Algebra.adjoin κ (Set.range t)) :=
1013	    Algebra.isMulCommutative_adjoin κ (by
1014	      rintro _ ⟨i, rfl⟩ _ ⟨j, rfl⟩; exact (hcomm i j).eq)
1015	  letI : CommRing (Algebra.adjoin κ (Set.range t)) := IsMulCommutative.instCommRing
1016	  simp only [polyEndHom, RingHom.comp_apply, AlgHom.toRingHom_eq_coe, RingHom.coe_coe,
1017	    MvPolynomial.aeval_C]
1018	  rw [Algebra.algebraMap_eq_smul_one]
1019	  simp
1020	
1021	/-- The `MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ`-module structure on `M` in which `X i` acts as `t i`,
1022	obtained by restricting scalars along `polyEndHom`. Project-local: the module over the free
1023	polynomial ring required by the ambient subquotient induction. -/
1024	noncomputable def polyModule {r : ℕ} (t : Fin r → Module.End κ M)
1025	    (hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)) : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ) M :=
1026	  Module.compHom M (polyEndHom t hcomm)
1027	
1028	/-- In the polynomial-module structure, `X i` acts as the endomorphism `t i`. -/
1029	lemma polyModule_X_smul {r : ℕ} (t : Fin r → Module.End κ M)
1030	    (hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)) (i : Fin r) (m : M) :
1031	    letI := polyModule t hcomm
1032	    (MvPolynomial.X i : MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ) • m = t i m := by
1033	  change polyEndHom t hcomm (MvPolynomial.X i) • m = t i m
1034	  rw [polyEndHom_X, Module.End.smul_def]
1035	
1036	/-- In the polynomial-module structure, a constant `C c` acts by the scalar `c`. -/
1037	lemma polyModule_C_smul {r : ℕ} (t : Fin r → Module.End κ M)
1038	    (hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)) (c : κ) (m : M) :
1039	    letI := polyModule t hcomm
1040	    (MvPolynomial.C c : MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ) • m = c • m := by
1041	  change polyEndHom t hcomm (MvPolynomial.C c) • m = c • m
1042	  rw [polyEndHom_C, Module.End.smul_def]
1043	  simp
1044	
1045	/-- The polynomial-module structure is compatible with the `κ`-action (scalar tower):
1046	the algebra map `κ → MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ` followed by the polynomial action recovers the
1047	original `κ`-action on `M`. -/
1048	lemma polyModule_isScalarTower {r : ℕ} (t : Fin r → Module.End κ M)
1049	    (hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)) :
1050	    letI := polyModule t hcomm
1051	    IsScalarTower κ (MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ) M := by
1052	  letI := polyModule t hcomm
1053	  refine IsScalarTower.of_algebraMap_smul fun c m => ?_
1054	  change polyEndHom t hcomm (algebraMap κ (MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ) c) • m = c • m
1055	  rw [MvPolynomial.algebraMap_eq, polyEndHom_C, Module.End.smul_def]
1056	  simp
1057	
1058	/-- A `κ`-submodule `N` that is stable under each commuting endomorphism `t i` is a
1059	`MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ`-submodule of `M` (same carrier), for the polynomial-module structure
1060	`polyModule`. Project-local: lifts an ambient `t`-stable submodule to the polynomial ring,
1061	keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of `M` (no derived carrier). -/
1062	noncomputable def polySubmodule {r : ℕ} (t : Fin r → Module.End κ M)
1063	    (hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)) (N : Submodule κ M)
1064	    (hN : ∀ i, N.map (t i) ≤ N) :
1065	    letI := polyModule t hcomm
1066	    Submodule (MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ) M :=
1067	  letI := polyModule t hcomm
1068	  { carrier := N
1069	    add_mem' := fun ha hb => N.add_mem ha hb
1070	    zero_mem' := N.zero_mem
1071	    smul_mem' := by
1072	      have key : ∀ (p : MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ), ∀ m ∈ N, p • m ∈ N := by
1073	        intro p
1074	        induction p using MvPolynomial.induction_on with
1075	        | C a => intro m hm; rw [polyModule_C_smul]; exact N.smul_mem a hm
1076	        | add p q hp hq => intro m hm; rw [add_smul]; exact N.add_mem (hp m hm) (hq m hm)
1077	        | mul_X p i hp =>
1078	            intro m hm
1079	            rw [mul_smul, polyModule_X_smul]
1080	            exact hp _ (hN i (Submodule.mem_map_of_mem hm))
1081	      intro p m hm
1082	      exact key p m hm }
1083	
1084	/-- The carrier of `polySubmodule` is the original `κ`-submodule. -/
1085	@[simp] lemma polySubmodule_coe {r : ℕ} (t : Fin r → Module.End κ M)
1086	    (hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)) (N : Submodule κ M)
1087	    (hN : ∀ i, N.map (t i) ≤ N) :
1088	    letI := polyModule t hcomm
1089	    ((polySubmodule t hcomm N hN : Submodule (MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ) M) : Set M) = N :=
1090	  rfl
1091	
1092	end PolyModule
1093	
1094	/-! ### The ambient subquotient datum
1095	
1096	Bundles a homogeneous pair `N' ≤ N` of a fixed graded `κ`-module `M = ⨁ ℳ n` with `r`
1097	pairwise-commuting degree-raising endomorphisms preserving the pair, plus the finiteness of
1098	the represented subquotient `N/N'` over the free polynomial ring `MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ` (via
1099	`polySubmodule`, so the underlying carriers stay ambient submodules of `M`). This is the
1100	length-`r` carrier of the Stacks 00K1 ambient induction (`def:graded_subquotientHilb`). -/
1101	
1102	section Datum
1103	
1104	variable {κ M : Type*} [Field κ] [AddCommGroup M] [Module κ M]
1105	variable (ℳ : ℕ → Submodule κ M) [DirectSum.Decomposition ℳ]
1106	
1107	/-- A length-`r` **ambient subquotient datum** over the fixed graded module `M = ⨁ ℳ n`.
1108	Project-local: the carrier of the Stacks 00K1 ambient induction (`def:graded_subquotientHilb`).
1109	The finiteness field `hfin` records that the represented subquotient `N/N'` is a finite module
1110	over the free polynomial ring `MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ` acting through the `t i`; the carriers
1111	involved are the ambient `t`-stable submodules `polySubmodule … N`, `polySubmodule … N'`. -/
1112	structure SubquotientDatum (r : ℕ) where
1113	  /-- The upper homogeneous submodule. -/
1114	  N : Submodule κ M
1115	  /-- The lower homogeneous submodule. -/
1116	  N' : Submodule κ M
1117	  /-- `N'` is contained in `N`. -/
1118	  hle : N' ≤ N
1119	  /-- `N` is homogeneous. -/
1120	  hN : N.IsHomogeneous ℳ
1121	  /-- `N'` is homogeneous. -/
1122	  hN' : N'.IsHomogeneous ℳ
1123	  /-- The `r` degree-raising endomorphisms. -/
1124	  t : Fin r → Module.End κ M
1125	  /-- They pairwise commute. -/
1126	  hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)
1127	  /-- Each raises degree by one. -/
1128	  hraise : ∀ i, RaisesDegree ℳ (t i)
1129	  /-- Each preserves `N`. -/
1130	  hpresN : ∀ i, N.map (t i) ≤ N
1131	  /-- Each preserves `N'`. -/
1132	  hpresN' : ∀ i, N'.map (t i) ≤ N'
1133	  /-- The represented subquotient `N/N'` is finite over `MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ`. -/
1134	  hfin : letI := polyModule t hcomm
1135	    Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ)
1136	      (↥(polySubmodule t hcomm N hpresN) ⧸
1137	        (polySubmodule t hcomm N' hpresN').comap (polySubmodule t hcomm N hpresN).subtype)
1138	
1139	/-- The ambient Hilbert function `n ↦ dim_κ(N ⊓ ℳ n) − dim_κ(N' ⊓ ℳ n)` of a subquotient
1140	datum (`def:graded_subquotientHilb`). -/
1141	noncomputable def SubquotientDatum.hilb {r : ℕ} (D : SubquotientDatum ℳ r) : ℕ → ℚ :=
1142	  subquotientHilb ℳ D.N D.N'
1143	
1144	/-- Base-case finiteness: a module finite over `MvPolynomial σ κ` for an *empty* index `σ` —
1145	in particular `σ = Fin 0`, the length-zero subquotient datum — is finite-dimensional over `κ`,
1146	since `MvPolynomial σ κ ≃ₐ[κ] κ`. Project-local: the base case of the Stacks 00K1 induction.
1147	Stated outside the `Datum` section as it needs no grading. -/
1148	lemma finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite
1149	    {κ : Type*} [Field κ] {σ : Type*} [IsEmpty σ]
1150	    {Q : Type*} [AddCommGroup Q] [Module κ Q]
1151	    [Module (MvPolynomial σ κ) Q] [IsScalarTower κ (MvPolynomial σ κ) Q]
1152	    [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial σ κ) Q] : FiniteDimensional κ Q := by
1153	  haveI : Module.Finite κ (MvPolynomial σ κ) :=
1154	    Module.Finite.equiv (MvPolynomial.isEmptyAlgEquiv κ σ).symm.toLinearEquiv
1155	  exact Module.Finite.trans (MvPolynomial σ κ) Q
1156	
1157	end Datum
1158	
1159	end GradedModule
1160	
1161	end AlgebraicGeometry
1162

954	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
955	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
956	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
957	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
958	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
959	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
960	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
961	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
962	  \begin{itemize}
963	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
964	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
965	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
966	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
967	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
968	    \item a finiteness condition: \(M\) is a finite module over the polynomial ring
969	      \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\).
970	  \end{itemize}
971	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
972	  Hilbert function} is
973	  \[
974	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
975	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
976	  \]
977	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
978	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
979	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
980	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
981	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
982	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
983	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
984	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
985	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
986	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
987	\end{definition}
988	
989	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
990	
991	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
992	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
993	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
994	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
995	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
996	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
997	
998	\begin{definition}\leanok
999	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1000	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1001	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1002	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1003	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1004	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1005	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1006	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1007	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1008	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1009	\end{definition}
1010	
1011	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1012	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1013	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1014	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1015	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1016	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1017	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1018	\end{lemma}
1019	
1020	\begin{proof}
1021	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1022	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1023	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1024	\end{proof}
1025	
1026	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1027	[Degree-shift commutation]
1028	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1029	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1030	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1031	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1032	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1033	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1034	  \[
1035	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1036	  \]
1037	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1038	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1039	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1040	\end{lemma}
1041	
1042	\begin{proof}
1043	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1044	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1045	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1046	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1047	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1048	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1049	\end{proof}
1050	
1051	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1052	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1053	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1054	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1055	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1056	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1057	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1058	\end{lemma}
1059	
1060	\begin{proof}
1061	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1062	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1063	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1064	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1065	\end{proof}
1066	
1067	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1068	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1069	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1070	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1071	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1072	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1073	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1074	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1075	\end{lemma}
1076	
1077	\begin{proof}
1078	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1079	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1080	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1081	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1082	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1083	  homogeneous.
1084	\end{proof}
1085	
1086	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1087	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1088	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1089	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1090	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1091	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1092	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1093	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1094	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1095	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1096	\end{lemma}
1097	
1098	\begin{proof}
1099	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1100	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1101	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1102	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1103	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1104	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1105	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1106	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1107	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1108	  homogeneous.
1109	\end{proof}
1110	
1111	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1112	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1113	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1115	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1116	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1117	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1118	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1119	  this is recorded here.
1120	\end{lemma}
1121	
1122	\begin{proof}
1123	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1124	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1125	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1126	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1127	\end{proof}
1128	
1129	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1130	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1131	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1133	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1134	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1135	\end{lemma}
1136	
1137	\begin{proof}
1138	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1139	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1140	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1141	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1142	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1143	\end{proof}
1144	
1145	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1146	[Ambient image identity]
1147	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1149	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1150	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1151	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1152	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1153	  \[
1154	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1155	  \]
1156	\end{lemma}
1157	
1158	\begin{proof}
1159	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1160	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1161	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1162	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1163	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1164	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1165	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1166	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1167	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1168	\end{proof}
1169	
1170	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1171	[Ambient distributive law]
1172	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1174	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1175	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1176	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1177	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1178	  \[
1179	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1180	  \]
1181	\end{lemma}
1182	
1183	\begin{proof}
1184	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1185	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1186	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1187	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1188	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1189	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1190	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1191	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1192	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1193	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1194	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1195	\end{proof}
1196	
1197	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1198	
1199	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1200	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1201	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_ker_coker}
1202	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1203	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1204	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1205	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1206	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1207	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1208	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1209	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1210	  \[
1211	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1212	    \qquad
1213	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1214	  \]
1215	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1216	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1217	  \begin{enumerate}
1218	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1219	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1220	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1221	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1222	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1223	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1224	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1225	  \end{enumerate}
1226	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1227	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1228	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1229	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1230	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1231	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1232	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1233	\end{lemma}
1234	
1235	\begin{proof}
1236	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1237	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1238	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1239	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1240	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1241	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1242	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1243	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1244	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1245	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1246	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1247	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1248	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1249	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1250	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1251	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1252	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1253	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1254	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1255	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1256	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1257	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1258	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1259	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1260	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1261	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1262	
1263	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1264	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1265	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1266	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1267	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1268	
1269	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1270	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1271	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1272	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1273	
1274	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1275	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1276	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1277	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1278	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1279	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1280	\end{proof}
1281	
1282	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1283	
1284	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1285	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1286	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1288	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1289	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1290	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1291	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1292	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1293	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1294	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1295	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1296	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1297	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1298	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1299	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1300	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1301	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1302	  \[
1303	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1304	  \]
1305	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1306	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1307	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1308	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1309	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1310	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1311	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1312	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1313	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1314	\end{lemma}
1315	
1316	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1317	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1318	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1319	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1320	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1321	%  following short exact sequence
1322	%  $$
1323	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1324	%  $$
1325	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1326	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1327	\begin{proof}
1328	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1329	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1330	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1331	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1332	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1333	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1334	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1335	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1336	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1337	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1338	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1339	
1340	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1341	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1342	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1343	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1344	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1345	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1346	
1347	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1348	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1349	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1350	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1351	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1352	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1353	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1354	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1355	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1356	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1357	
1358	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1359	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1360	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1361	  \[
1362	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1363	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1364	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1365	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1366	  \]
1367	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1368	\end{proof}

1370	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1371	
1372	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness transfer for the subquotients]
1373	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1374	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1375	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1376	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1377	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r \ge 1\)
1378	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), so that \(M\) is finite over
1379	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Then the two
1380	  subquotients
1381	  \[
1382	    K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N'), \qquad C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)
1383	  \]
1384	  of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} are finite modules over
1385	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\); that is, they are subquotient data of length
1386	  \(r-1\), satisfying the finiteness condition. This is what is needed for the
1387	  inductive hypothesis of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}.
1388	
1389	  The kernel \(K\) and cokernel \(C\) of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1390	  satisfy the same conditions as the original datum -- a homogeneous pair, pairwise-commuting
1391	  degree-raising endomorphisms preserving the pair, a polynomial-ring module structure, and
1392	  the finiteness condition -- but of length \(r-1\) rather than \(r\).
1393	\end{lemma}
1394	
1395	\begin{proof}
1396	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1397	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1398	  \emph{The polynomial module structure on \(M\).} The \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1399	  degree-raising endomorphisms \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\) generate a \(\kappa\)-subalgebra
1400	  \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\}\) of the \emph{noncommutative}
1401	  endomorphism algebra \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); because the generators
1402	  commute pairwise, \(A\) is commutative (\cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}).
1403	  Commutativity of \(A\) is exactly what makes the evaluation homomorphism at the
1404	  generators a legal \(\kappa\)-algebra homomorphism \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1405	  \to A\): evaluation refuses the noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) as a
1406	  target. Composing with the inclusion \(A \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1407	  and restricting scalars along it endows \(M\) with the structure of a module over
1408	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action and
1409	  characterised by \(t_i \cdot m = x_i m\).
1410	
1411	  \emph{Transfer down one generator.} Set \(A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and
1412	  \(x = t_{r-1}\). Since \(M\) is finite over the Noetherian ring \(A\), \(M\) is a
1413	  Noetherian \(A\)-module, so the submodule \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq M\) is
1414	  finite over \(A\) and the quotients \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\) and
1415	  \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) are finite over \(A\). By
1416	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} the element \(x = t_{r-1}\) annihilates
1417	  both subquotients, so the \(A\)-action on each factors through the surjection of
1418	  polynomial rings
1419	  \[
1420	    A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]
1421	  \]
1422	  fixing \(t_i\) for \(i < r-1\) and sending \(t_{r-1} \mapsto 0\). Applying the
1423	  finite-generation transfer along a surjection
1424	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}) turns finiteness over \(A\)
1425	  into finiteness over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), supplying the length-\((r-1)\)
1426	  finiteness witnesses for \(K\) and \(C\).
1427	
1428	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1429	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), not the subalgebra
1430	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-2}\} \subseteq
1431	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1432	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection that
1433	  the scalar transfer requires. Keeping free polynomial rings at every step keeps the
1434	  surjection -- and hence the transfer -- available throughout. Everything is finite
1435	  generation of ambient submodules of the fixed \(M\) and their quotients.
1436	\end{proof}
1437	
1438	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1439	
1440	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1441	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1442	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1444	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1445	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1446	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1447	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1448	  \[
1449	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1450	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1451	  \]
1452	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
1453	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
1454	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
1455	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
1456	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
1457	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
1458	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
1459	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1460	  \[
1461	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
1462	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1463	  \]
1464	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1465	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1466	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1467	\end{lemma}
1468	
1469	\begin{proof}
1470	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1471	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1472	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1473	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1474	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1475	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1476	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1477	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1478	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1479	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1480	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1481	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1482	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1483	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
1484	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
1485	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
1486	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1487	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
1488	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
1489	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1490	\end{proof}

1492	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
1493	
1494	\begin{lemma}[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
1495	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
1496	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
1497	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1498	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1499	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1500	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump}
1501	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1502	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1503	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1504	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
1505	  degree-one generators).}
1506	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
1507	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
1508	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
1509	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1510	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
1511	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
1512	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
1513	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
1514	  \(M\).
1515	\end{lemma}
1516	
1517	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
1518	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1519	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
1520	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
1521	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
1522	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
1523	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
1524	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
1525	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
1526	\begin{proof}
1527	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1528	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1529	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1530	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump}
1531	  We induct on the length \(r\).
1532	
1533	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} With no endomorphisms, the finiteness witness says
1534	  \(M\) is a finite module over \(\kappa[\,] = \kappa\), so \(M\) is a
1535	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space. Hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) and
1536	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\), so \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for
1537	  \(n \gg 0\). By \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is
1538	  rational of order \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
1539	
1540	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
1541	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
1542	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
1543	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), with valid
1544	  finiteness witnesses supplied by the finiteness transfer
1545	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The induction hypothesis applied
1546	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
1547	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
1548	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1549	  \[
1550	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
1551	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
1552	  \]
1553	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
Let me continue reading the rest of the blueprint.
Let me read the remaining parts of the blueprint.

1554	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
1555	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
1556	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
1557	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
1558	\end{proof}

1560	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1561	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1562	
1563	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1564	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1565	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1566	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1567	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
1568	
1569	\begin{definition}
1570	
1571	\leanok
1572	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
1573	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
1574	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
1575	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1576	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
1577	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
1578	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
1579	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
1580	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1581	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1582	  % Polynomials" (read from
1583	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1584	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1585	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
1586	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
1587	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
1588	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
1589	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1590	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
1591	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
1592	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
1593	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
1594	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
1595	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
1596	  \(U \subseteq X\),
1597	  \[
1598	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1599	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
1600	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
1601	  \]
1602	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
1603	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
1604	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
1605	
1606	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
1607	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
1608	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
1609	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
1610	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
1611	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
1612	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
1613	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
1614	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
1615	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
1616	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
1617	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1618	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
1619	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
1620	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
1621	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1622	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
1623	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
1624	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
1625	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
1626	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
1627	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
1628	
1629	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
1630	\end{definition}
1631	
1632	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1633	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
1634	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
1635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
1636	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
1637	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
1638	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
1639	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
1640	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
1641	  \(U\):
1642	  \[
1643	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1644	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
1645	  \]
1646	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
1647	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
1648	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
1649	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
1650	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
1651	\end{lemma}
1652	
1653	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1654	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1655	  localization]
1656	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1657	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1658	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
1659	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1660	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1661	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1662	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1663	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1664	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1665	  \[
1666	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1667	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1668	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1669	  \]
1670	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1671	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
1672	
1673	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
1674	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1675	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1676	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1677	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1678	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
1679	\end{lemma}
1680	
1681	\begin{proof}
1682	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
1683	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
1684	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
1685	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
1686	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
1687	
1688	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
1689	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
1690	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
1691	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
1692	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
1693	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
1694	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
1695	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
1696	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
1697	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
1698	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
1699	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
1700	\end{proof}
1701	
1702	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
1703	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1704	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
1705	  \mathlibok
1706	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
1707	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
1708	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
1709	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
1710	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
1711	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
1712	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
1713	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
1714	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
1715	\end{lemma}
1716	
1717	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
1718	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1719	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
1720	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1721	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
1722	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
1723	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
1724	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1725	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
1726	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
1727	  %  associated to $M$.
1728	  %  [...]
1729	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
1730	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
1731	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
1732	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
1733	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
1734	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
1735	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
1736	  \begin{enumerate}
1737	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
1738	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
1739	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
1740	    \item the section-restriction map
1741	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
1742	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
1743	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
1744	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
1745	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
1746	  \end{enumerate}
1747	\end{lemma}
1748	
1749	\begin{proof}
1750	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1751	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
1752	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
1753	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
1754	
1755	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
1756	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
1757	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
1758	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
1759	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
1760	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
1761	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
1762	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
1763	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
1764	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
1765	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
1766	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
1767	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
1768	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
1769	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
1770	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
1771	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
1772	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
1773	\end{proof}

1776	
1777	\leanok
1778	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
1779	  \label{def:schematic_support}
1780	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
1781	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator, lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le,
1782	    def:schematic_support_immersion}
1783	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1784	  % Polynomials" (read from
1785	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1786	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1787	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,

1789	  %  be any coherent sheaf on

1791	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}

1793	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
1794	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
1795	  \[
1796	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
1797	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
1798	  \]

1800	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
1801	  \(X\).

1804	\begin{definition}\leanok

1810	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
1811	  \[

1813	  \]
1814	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient

1819	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.

1822	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
1823	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
1824	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
1825	  \mathlibok
1826	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
1827	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
1828	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
1829	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
1830	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
1831	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
1832	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
1833	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
1834	\end{lemma}

1842	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
1843	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
1844	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from

1846	  % + L418-L419).
1847	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1848	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
1849	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
1850	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
1851	  %  [...]
1852	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
1853	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
1854	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1855	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
1856	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
1857	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism

1893	\section{The Quot functor}
1894	\label{sec:quot_functor}
1895	
1896	\begin{definition}\leanok
1897	[Quot functor]
1898	  \label{def:quot_functor}
1899	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
1900	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
1901	
1902	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
1903	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
1904	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
1905	  % + L481-L498).
1906	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1907	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
1908	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
1909	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
1910	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
1911	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
1912	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
1913	  %
1914	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
1915	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
1916	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
1917	  %
1918	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
1919	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
1920	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
1921	  %
1922	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
1923	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
1924	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
1925	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
1926	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
1927	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
1928	  %  well-defined, which gives
1929	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
1930	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
1931	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
1932	  %  [...]
1933	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
1934	  %  as a co-product
1935	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
1936	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
1937	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
1938	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
1939	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
1940	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
1941	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
1942	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1943	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1944	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
1945	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
1946	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
1947	  \[
1948	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
1949	  \]
1950	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
1951	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
1952	  \begin{enumerate}
1953	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
1954	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
1955	      which is flat over \(T\);
1956	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
1957	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
1958	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
1959	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
1960	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
1961	      \(\Phi\).
1962	  \end{enumerate}
1963	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
1964	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
1965	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
1966	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
1967	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
1968	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
1969	\end{definition}

1971	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
1972	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
1973	
1974	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
1975	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
1976	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
1977	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
1978	
1979	\begin{definition}\leanok
1980	[Grassmannian scheme]
1981	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
1982	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
1983	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}

2109	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding

2122	\begin{proof}
2123	  
2124	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
2125	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
2126	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
2127	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
2128	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
2129	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
2130	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
2131	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
2132	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
2133	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
2134	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
2135	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
2136	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
2137	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
2138	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
2139	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
2140	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
2141	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
2142	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
2143	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
2144	
2145	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
2146	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
2147	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
2148	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
2149	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
2150	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
2151	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
2152	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
2153	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
2154	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
2155	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
2156	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
2157	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
2158	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
2159	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
2160	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
2161	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
2162	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
2163	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
2164	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
2165	\end{proof}
